config.title: The Impact of Increasing Aridity and Dryland Expansion on Soils, Humans and Nature
config.abstract: Over the last 30 years, most regions have experienced rising aridity. As a result drylands have expanded considerably, impacting arable lands, agricultural production and livelihoods. Higher aridity also puts pressure on the habitats of plant and animal species. Setting aside protected areas can help drier areas to remain productive ecologically, but protected areas can conflict with agricultural expansion to guarantee global food security.
config.citation: Maarten Lambrechts. 2026. “100 Years of Past, Present and Future Challenges in Tackling Extreme Poverty” In Atlas of Global Development 2026, edited by A. F. Pirlea, U. Serajuddin, A. Thudt, D. Wadhwa, and M. Welch. Washington, DC: World Bank. License: Creative Commons Attribution CC BY 3.0 IGO. https://doi.org/10.60616/f7gk-me57

content.text: Land degradation

Land is the terrestrial system composed of soils, water resources and living organisms, including the complex interactions between these three components. Healthy land provides vital services to humans and the environment: it offers fertile soils, allowing high agricultural productivity and guaranteeing food security, it acts as a water buffer by preventing floods and supplying stored fresh water in times of drought, it provides habitats for thriving ecosystems, and it acts as a carbon sink, storing twice the amount of carbon in the atmosphere.

But in many areas human activities and natural conditions are negatively impacting the health and productivity of land. Land degradation is the process of cropland, pastures, forests and woodlands becoming less healthy and less biologically and economically productive. Human led changes in land, like deforestation and urban sprawl, intensive agricultural practices and changing climatic conditions all contribute to land degradation.

content.label: of additional land is affected by land degradation each year [reference: unccd23]

content.text: Land degradation has a negative impact on food security, as degraded land has a lower agricultural productivity, or can even be lost for agricultural use altogether. So it leads to lower availability of food and higher food prices. Livelihoods of rural households that rely on agriculture for work are adversely impacted through loss of income.

Additionally, degraded land has a lower capacity to sustain healthy ecosystems, resulting in a negative impact on biodiversity. Land degradation also disrupts water cycles, affecting the provisioning of fresh water in times of drought and the capacity to store water during wetter periods.
Degradation of arable land

More than a quarter of all land is dedicated to the production of livestock: 32 million square kilometers of grazing land, and 6 million square kilometers used for growing animal feed. 10 million square kilometers of land is dedicated to growing crops for human consumption, producing 82 percent of the global human diet (measured in calories).

The bulk of the land used for growing crops for human consumption is arable land: land used for cultivating annual crops, with wheat, maize, rice and soybeans being the most important ones. A smaller portion of this land lies under permanent crops, like orchards and plantations.

content.title: Arable lands occupy 14 million square kilometers
content.subtitle: Equal to 9.4 percent of the global land surface

content.text: With the global population projected to peak to more than 10 billion people by the 2080s, increasing the productivity of agricultural land is imperative for food security. But arable lands are suffering from multiple, overlapping land degradation processes.

content.title: Four processes degrading arable land
content.salinization: Share of arable land affected by salinization
content.carbon_decline: Share of arable land affected by carbon decline
content.erosion: Share of arable land affected by erosion
content.aridity: Share of arable land affected by aridity
content.visdescription: World map showing the distribution of arable lands, and of the salinization, loss of organic carbon, erosion and aridity degradation processes.

scenes.text: Global arable lands cover 14.2 million square kilometers, 1.5 times the land area of the United States or China. This land is used for the production of food for human consumption, of animal feed and of non-food crops like biofuels.

[emphasis: Salinization] is the process of accumulation of salts in the soil. It reduces plant growth, and can even make land unsuitable for agriculture. It is mostly caused by irrigation practices with insufficient drainage. Salinization affects almost 2 percent of arable lands, mostly in drier and coastal areas.

Organic carbon in the soil makes it fertile: it retains nutrients and water for plants, and makes it structurally stable. Agricultural practices like heavy tilling and the removal or burning of crop residues leads to a [emphasis: decline in soil organic carbon]. This is affecting 3.3 percent of arable land.

A more important factor in the degradation of arable land is [emphasis: soil erosion]. Soil erosion happens when rainfall or water runoff detaches and carries the top soil. Natural factors determining the risk of soil erosion are the amount and intensity of rainfall, topography and soil characteristics. Human factors are excessive or poorly timed tillage, monocropping and providing inadequate cover crops. Soil erosion affects 30 percent of all arable land.

But by far the largest pressure for these agricultural systems is [emphasis: aridity]. Water resources are a subsystem of land, and under arid conditions they put constraints on soils and vegetation, the two other components of land. This causes land degradation, defined as a reduction in the agro-ecological productivity of land. 55% of arable land is situated in drylands, which have expanded and become more arid over the last decades.

content.text: Only 22 percent of arable land isn't experiencing pressure from any of the four land degradation processes discussed above. More than 10 percent of arable land is threatened by at least 2 of these processes [reference: pravalie2021].

Adopting agricultural practices like incorporating plant residues into the soil, improved irrigation, reduced tillage and agroforestry (the integration of trees and shrubs into crop systems) all can contribute to stopping and reversing the land degradation processes of salinization, declining soil organic carbon, and soil erosion. But these measures alone won't counter the most widespread cause of degradation of arable lands: rising aridity.
Aridity

In contrast to drought, which is defined as a short-term, lower-than-normal amount of precipitation, the aridity of a region is a characteristic of its climate over the longer term. In a region with high aridity, rainfall is low compared to the evaporation of water driven by heat, sun, wind and plant transpiration.
Humid areas have more than two thirds of their potential evaporation replenished by precipitation. In these areas, plant growth is not limited by the availability of water.

When average precipitation is less than two thirds of potential evaporation, an area is classified as dryland [footnote: Aridity is measured with the Aridity Index, which is the ratio between precipitation and potential evapotranspiration. When an area has an Aridity Index lower than 0.65 it is considered to be dryland]. Drylands are further classified as dry subhumid, semi-arid, arid or hyperarid. Together, drylands cover 41 percent of global lands (excluding Antarctica) [reference: aridityreport].

content.title: Global distribution of aridity classes
content.visdescription: World map showing the distribution of humid, dry subhumid, semi-arid, arid and hyperarid regions around the world.

content.text: More than three quarters of all land on Earth experienced a more arid climate during the 1990-2020 period than during 1960-1990. Some smaller areas experienced a more humid climate. [reference: aridityreport]

content.title: Most regions are getting more arid
content.subtitle: Change in aridity between 1960-1990 and 1990-2020
content.visdescription: World map showing where aridity increased in brown and where aridity decreased in green. Brown is dominating the map.
content.keyFactLabel: of all land experienced a more arid climate than 30 years ago.

content.text: The likely culprit behind the increasing aridity is human-caused climate change [reference: aridityreport]. Climate change leads to higher aridity in two ways. Rising temperatures increase evaporation, which, with the same amount of precipitation, leads to more arid conditions. And climate change also brings shifts in precipitation patterns, changing where, when and how much it rains.

As a result, many areas shifted to a drier aridity classification. Together, drylands now span an additional 4.3 million square kilometers compared to 30 years ago, an area equal to twice the size of Greenland. Of these new drylands, one fifth (0.9 million square kilometers) are arable lands.

content.title: 4.3 million km2 humid areas turned to drylands since 30 years ago
content.subtitle: Areas that were humid during the 1960-1990 period that are now classified as drylands.
content.visdescription: A world map showing the areas that converted to drylands in the 1990 - 2020 period compared to the 1960-1990 period
content.keyFactLabel: km2 of humid areas turned to drylands since 30 years ago.

content.text: Hotspots showing significant dryland expansion include the western United States, the Yucatan Peninsula, north-eastern Brazil, north-western Argentina, the entire Mediterranean region, the northern side of the Black Sea, the Sahel, the Rift Valley, north-eastern South Africa, the bordering area between Russia and Kazakhstan, large parts of Mongolia and north-eastern China and south-eastern Australia.

In 1990, 1.2 billion people, or 22.5 percent of the global population, were living in drylands. Population growth and the expansion of drylands made these numbers rise to 2.3 billion and 30.9 percent in 2020, and the number of people living in drylands is expected to rise even more into the future. Depending on the population growth and climate change scenarios used, this number could grow to between 2.5 and 5 billion by 2100.
Impact of rising aridity

The impact of land degradation processes on arable land, of which aridity is the most important, are directly felt by agricultural communities: lower agricultural production leads to lower incomes and more food insecurity.

By 2040, the current area dedicated to the production of wheat is projected to produce 21 million tons less due to rising aridity, a reduction by 2.6 percent compared to 2023. The projections for other major crops are similar: without undertaking actions to combat aridification, the world will produce 20 million tonnes less of maize (-1.6%), 19 million tonnes less of rice (-2.4%) and 8 million tonnes less of soybeans (-2.2%) [reference: malpede23].
Sub-saharan Africa, with its high dependency on agriculture and severe impact of aridification, could lose up to 10% of GDP per capita by 2040. [reference: malpede25]

But the impacts of increased aridity go beyond these direct impacts, and cascade down as chain reactions through interconnected natural and socioeconomic systems, ultimately resulting in negative consequences for human health and well being. On top of increased food insecurity, other events with a negative impact on human health and well being linked to aridity include water scarcity, wildfires and sand and dust storms.

Rising aridity and drought are considered significant factors affecting fast-increasing rates of human migration around the world, particularly in the arid areas of Southern Europe, the Middle East and North Africa and Southern Asia [reference: hoffman].
Aridity and biodiversity
Aridity and biodiversity are inversely related: lower aridity, that is, higher water availability can support more plant and animal life. So the expansion of drylands negatively impacts biodiversity.

Ecosystems are highly sensitive to changes in aridity. When an area becomes drier and its aridity nears certain threshold values, even small changes in aridity can provoke big, systemic changes in healthy ecosystems.

When a healthy ecosystem is exposed to more arid conditions, it will first enter a [emphasis: vegetation decline phase]: plants react to protect themselves from drying out, but will have less capacity for photosynthesis as a result. Plants grow slower, and provide a smaller base for the organisms higher in the food pyramid. The (fewer) species able to thrive in drier conditions (e.g., grasses) outcompete less drought-tolerant species (like many tree species), and forests turn to grasslands.

When aridity keeps increasing, ecosystems will experience a [emphasis: soil disruption phase], in which the levels of vital components of healthy and functional soils, like organic carbon, total nitrogen, clay content and symbiotic fungi beneficial to plants decrease abruptly. Grasslands turn to shrublands.
Even more arid conditions lead to [emphasis: systemic breakdown], in which very few species manage to survive.

content.title: Consequences of increased aridity on ecosystems and land degradation
content.visdescription: An illustration showing the sequence of the vegetation decline phase, the soil disruption phase and the systemic breakdown at certain aridity thresholds.

content.text: All plant and animal species are constrained by their range on the aridity spectrum: they have a minimum and maximum aridity in which they can survive. When aridity rises, conditions in an ecosystem can be pushed outside of a species' aridity range. In that case, a species loses its habitat, and can only survive by migrating to other areas where its climatic requirements are still met, or it has to adapt to the drier conditions.

Under a high-emission climate scenario, more than half of all mammal, reptile, fish, amphibian and bird species living on land could lose their local habitat to unprecedented aridity by 2100. But even under a low-emission climate scenario, the share of affected species is still 25 percent [reference: liu].

content.title: Where species are projected to lose their local habitat due to rising aridity
content.subtitle: Hotspots with the highest shares of terrestrial animals losing local habitats include the west coast of Africa, west Australia and the Iberian Peninsula, but also humid regions such as southern Mexico and the northern Amazon rainforest
content.share_species: Share of vertebrate species losing local habitat
content.visdescription: A world map showing where vertebrate species can lose their local habitat due to rising aridity under 3 greenhouse gas emission scenarios.
content.keyFactLabel: of vertebrate species could lose local habitats because of rising aridity by 2100.

content.text: Even in a low-emission climate scenario, more than 3 percent of animal species could not lose only some of their local habitats: because conditions will be too dry in all the areas where they are living today, they risk losing all of their habitats. Especially reptiles (4.4 percent) and amphibians (7.4 percent) could see their ecological niches completely dry up to unlivable conditions.
Protected areas

Protected areas are areas in which human presence and the extraction of resources are limited. When these areas cover the full ranges of species habitats, and when they are effectively managed, protected areas have proven to be able to safeguard habitats and populations of species under pressure. They can shield plant and animal species from habitat loss caused by land use change and other processes like rising aridity.

In December 2022, the 196 member countries of the Convention of Biological Diversity adopted the Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework. Target 3 of the Framework aims to designate 30% of Earth's land and ocean area as protected areas by 2030. This target, also known as 30 by 30, seems ambitious given that the global land area under protected status only increased from 15% in 2013 to 16.5% in 2024.

content.title: The share of global protected lands needs to double to reach the 30% goal
content.y_axis_title: Share of land that is protected
content.source: Retrieved from World Development Indicators (ER.LND.PTLD.ZS)
content.visdescription: A line chart showing the global terrestrial protected areas as share of the total land area. Compared the the 30% target, the line is mostly flat.

content.text: Drylands especially can benefit from protection: protected drylands are able to remain productive (remain "green" and support plant growth) much longer under increasingly arid conditions than less protected or unprotected areas. They "delay" the thresholds for the soil disruption and systemic breakdown phases [reference: delgado].

content.title: Protected areas remain productive under increasingly dry conditions
content.visdescription: A schematic line chart, with increasing aridity on the x axis and ecosystem productivity on the y axis. One line represents unprotected areas and one line represents areas under high protection. With increasing aridity, the line for highly protected areas remains well above the ecosystem productivity of the unprotected areas.

content.text: Unfortunately, this only holds for drylands with the highest protection status (strict nature reserves, wilderness areas and national parks). Less protected drylands do not show this higher resistance to aridity. Currently, only 7.1 percent of all drylands have a protection status at the highest level. Granting high levels of protection to more dryland areas could result in more resilient dryland ecosystems.

But there is a catch in protecting more drylands. Agricultural expansion in the past two decades has mostly occurred in drylands. Dryland productivity under high protection will be more resilient to future drying conditions, but at the same time, more food is needed to feed the ever-growing human population. There is no easy answer to the question of whether more highly protected areas to preserve dryland productivity should be set up, or whether agriculture should keep expanding into drylands to feed more people [reference: wang].
